0.text: CFI 057/2025 Clyde & Co LLP v (1) Union Properties P.J.S.C. (2) UPP Capital Investment LLC

1.text: FEBRUARY 02, 2026 COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE - ORDERS

3.text: IN THE DUBAI INTERNATIONAL FINANCIAL CENTRE COURTS

4.text: IN THE COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE

12.text: ORDER WITH REASONS OF H.E. JUSTICE ROGER STEWART KC

13.text: UPON the Claimant’s Claim Form and Particulars of Claim dated 10 June 2025 (the “Claim”)

14.text: AND UPON the Order with Reasons of H.E. Justice Roger Stewart KC dated 9 December 2025 (the “Order”)

15.text: AND UPON the Defendant’s Appeal Notice dated 31 December 2025 (the “Permission to Appeal Application”) seeking permission to appeal the Order

16.text: AND UPON the Claimant’s submissions opposing the Permission to Appeal Application

17.text: AND PURSUANT TO the Rules of the DIFC Courts (“RDC”)

19.text: 1. The Permission to Appeal Application is refused.

20.text: 2. The Defendant should pay the Claimant’s costs of the application for the Permission to Appeal Application to be assessed as follows:

21.text: (a) The Claimant is to fi le and serve a Statement of Costs and any short submissions in support of the cost claimed within 21 days of the date of this Order;

22.text: (b) The Defendant shall provide any submissions in response to the costs claimed within 14 days of the service of the Statement of Costs; and

23.text: (c) The Claimant is to provide any submissions in reply to the Defendant’s response within 7 days of service of that response.

24.text: (d) The quantum of costs to be paid by the Defendant to the Claimant will thereafter be assessed on the papers.

25.text:
Issued by:   
Delvin Sumo   
Assistant Registrar   
Date of Issue: 2 February 2026   
At: 11am

27.text: 1. This is an application by the Defendant for permission to appeal against that part of my Order with Reasons dated 9 December 2025 which granted declarations as to the meaning of the PSA substantial...

28.text: 2. The test for the grant of permission to appeal is that the Court must consider that the appeal would have a real prospect of success or there is some other compelling reason why the appeal should b...

29.text: 3. Judgment on the issues of construction in favour of the Claimant was given pursuant to RDC 24.1(b) which required that I considered that the Defendant had no real prospect of defending the issues o...

30.text: 4. The conclusions which I reached in relation to matters of construction were, in my view, clear once the rival construction arguments were considered. I have considered carefully the three grounds o...

31.text: (a) Ground 1 alleges that the construction I reached lacked commercial common sense with the result that a different construction should have been reached. I do not agree. The bargain made was unique ...

32.text: (b) Ground 2 alleges that there was a misconstruction of the termination provisions on the basis of what is said to be “boiler-plate” provisions. The relevant language was, however, completely clear. ...

33.text: (c) Ground 3 seeks to rely on a “contra proferentum” rule. There is no room for this rule where the terms of the agreement are clear.
